summary:
Claim: Numbness on shaving after parotidectomy results from auriculotemporal nerve injury.  
Evidence: The auriculotemporal nerve innervates the temporal region, including areas affected by shaving; it commonly injures during parotid dissection due to proximity to the facial nerve.  
Answer: B  

This conclusion corrects common misconceptions—such as attributing sensory loss to the facial nerve—which governs motor function only. By linking symptom location (shaving-related temporoparietal sensation) to precise anatomical supply, clinicians can accurately diagnose post-surgical neuropathy. Reinforcement via visual aids, clinical pearls, and case examples strengthens retention and prevents diagnostic errors. A structured teaching approach ensures residents recognize sensory deficits as distinct from motor impairments, improving both accuracy and confidence in managing post-parotidectomy complications.
